🎬 Netflix Content Analysis & Recommendation System¶

A Complete End-to-End Data Analysis Portfolio Project¶

Dataset Source: Netflix Movies and TV Shows – Kaggle
Author: Data Analyst Portfolio Project
Tools: Python · Pandas · NumPy · Matplotlib · Seaborn · Scikit-learn

📌 Project Overview¶

This notebook performs a full exploratory data analysis on Netflix's content library, uncovers business insights about content trends, and builds a content-based recommendation system.

Table of Contents¶

  1. Environment Setup & Data Loading
  2. Data Understanding & Cleaning
  3. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
  4. Trend Analysis
  5. Advanced Visualizations
  6. Business Insights & Conclusions
  7. Content-Based Recommendation System
  8. Future Improvements

1. Environment Setup & Data Loading ¶

In [27]:
# ── Core Libraries ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# ── Visualization ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
import seaborn as sns
from matplotlib.gridspec import GridSpec

# ── Machine Learning ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity

# ── Plot Styling ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
plt.rcParams.update({
    'figure.facecolor': '#0f0f0f',
    'axes.facecolor':   '#1a1a1a',
    'axes.edgecolor':   '#333333',
    'axes.labelcolor':  '#e0e0e0',
    'text.color':       '#e0e0e0',
    'xtick.color':      '#aaaaaa',
    'ytick.color':      '#aaaaaa',
    'grid.color':       '#2a2a2a',
    'grid.linestyle':   '--',
    'grid.alpha':        0.6,
    'font.family':      'DejaVu Sans',
    'font.size':         11,
    'axes.titlesize':    14,
    'axes.titleweight': 'bold',
    'axes.titlecolor':  '#ffffff',
})

NETFLIX_RED  = '#E50914'
ACCENT_GOLD  = '#f5c518'
ACCENT_TEAL  = '#00b4d8'
PALETTE      = [NETFLIX_RED, ACCENT_TEAL, ACCENT_GOLD, '#8338ec', '#06d6a0', '#fb8500']

print("✅ Libraries loaded successfully.")

✅ Libraries loaded successfully.
In [10]:
# ── Load Dataset ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Download from: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/shivamb/netflix-shows
# Place 'netflix_titles.csv' in the same directory as this notebook.
import pandas as pd 
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
df = pd.read_csv('netflix_titles.csv')

print(f"✅ Dataset loaded: {df.shape[0]:,} rows × {df.shape[1]} columns")

# ── Missing Value Analysis ────────────────────────────────────────────────────

NETFLIX_RED = "#E50914"

missing = df.isnull().sum()
missing_pct = (missing / len(df) * 100).round(2)

missing_df = pd.DataFrame({'Missing Count': missing, 'Missing %': missing_pct})
missing_df = missing_df[missing_df['Missing Count'] > 0].sort_values('Missing %', ascending=False)

print(missing_df.to_string())

# Visualize missing data
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4))
bars = ax.barh(missing_df.index, missing_df['Missing %'], color=NETFLIX_RED, edgecolor='none')
ax.set_xlabel('Missing %')
ax.set_title('Missing Data by Column')
ax.xaxis.grid(True)
for bar, val in zip(bars, missing_df['Missing %']):
    ax.text(val + 0.3, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
            f'{val}%', va='center', fontsize=9, color='#aaa')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

            Missing Count  Missing %
director             2634      29.91
country               831       9.44
cast                  825       9.37
date_added             10       0.11
rating                  4       0.05
duration                3       0.03
In [13]:
# ── Handle Missing Values ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# 1. director / cast / country → fill with 'Unknown'
for col in ['director', 'cast', 'country']:
    df[col].fillna('Unknown', inplace=True)

# 2. date_added → fill with 'Unknown' (will be excluded from date-based analysis)
df['date_added'].fillna('Unknown', inplace=True)

# 3. rating → fill with mode (most frequent)
df['rating'].fillna(df['rating'].mode()[0], inplace=True)

# 4. duration → fill with mode per type
df['duration'].fillna(
    df.groupby('type')['duration'].transform(lambda x: x.mode()[0]),
    inplace=True
)

# 5. description → fill with empty string (for NLP later)
df['description'].fillna('', inplace=True)

print(f"Missing values after cleaning: {df.isnull().sum().sum()}")

Missing values after cleaning: 4307

Such inplace method never works to update the original DataFrame or Series, because the intermediate object on which we are setting values always behaves as a copy (due to Copy-on-Write).

For example, when doing 'df[col].method(value, inplace=True)', try using 'df.method({col: value}, inplace=True)' instead, to perform the operation inplace on the original object, or try to avoid an inplace operation using 'df[col] = df[col].method(value)'.

In [14]:
# ── Remove Duplicates ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
before = len(df)
df.drop_duplicates(subset='show_id', inplace=True)
df.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)
print(f"Removed {before - len(df)} duplicate rows. Remaining: {len(df):,}")

Removed 0 duplicate rows. Remaining: 8,807
In [15]:
# ── Feature Engineering ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# 1. Parse date_added → datetime, extract year & month
df['date_added'] = df['date_added'].replace('Unknown', np.nan)
df['date_added_parsed'] = pd.to_datetime(df['date_added'], format='%B %d, %Y', errors='coerce')
df['year_added']  = df['date_added_parsed'].dt.year.astype('Int64')
df['month_added'] = df['date_added_parsed'].dt.month.astype('Int64')

# 2. Extract numeric duration
df['duration_int'] = df['duration'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(float)

# 3. Primary genre (first listed)
df['primary_genre'] = df['listed_in'].str.split(',').str[0].str.strip()

# 4. Clean listed_in into genre list
df['genres_list'] = df['listed_in'].str.split(',')
df['genres_list'] = df['genres_list'].apply(lambda x: [g.strip() for g in x])

print("✅ Feature engineering complete.")
df[['title', 'type', 'date_added_parsed', 'year_added', 'duration_int', 'primary_genre']].head(5)

✅ Feature engineering complete.
Out[15]:

2 Ganglands TV Show 2021-09-24 2021 1.0 Crime TV Shows
3 Jailbirds New Orleans TV Show 2021-09-24 2021 1.0 Docuseries
4 Kota Factory TV Show 2021-09-24 2021 2.0 International TV Shows

In [20]:
# ── Filter suspicious ratings ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Some rows have duration in 'rating' column — swap them
mask = df['rating'].str.contains('min|Season', na=False)
df.loc[mask, 'duration'] = df.loc[mask, 'rating']
df.loc[mask, 'rating']   = np.nan
df['rating'].fillna(df['rating'].mode()[0], inplace=True)

print(f"Unique ratings: {df['rating'].unique()}")
print(f"\n✅ Data cleaning complete. Final shape: {df.shape}")

Unique ratings: <StringArray>
[   'PG-13',    'TV-MA',       'PG',    'TV-14',    'TV-PG',     'TV-Y',
    'TV-Y7',        'R',     'TV-G',        'G',    'NC-17',        nan,
       'NR', 'TV-Y7-FV',       'UR']
Length: 15, dtype: str

✅ Data cleaning complete. Final shape: (8807, 18)

3. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) ¶

3.1 Content Type Distribution¶

In [21]:
ACCENT_TEAL= "#00b4d8"
ACCENT_GOLD = "#f5c518"
type_counts = df['type'].value_counts()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))
fig.suptitle('Content Type Distribution on Netflix', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold', color='white', y=1.02)

# Pie chart
wedge_props = dict(width=0.55, edgecolor='#0f0f0f', linewidth=3)
axes[0].pie(
    type_counts,
    labels=type_counts.index,
    autopct='%1.1f%%',
    colors=[NETFLIX_RED, ACCENT_TEAL],
    wedgeprops=wedge_props,
    textprops={'color': 'white', 'fontsize': 13}
)
axes[0].set_title('Share by Type', color='white')

# Bar chart
bars = axes[1].bar(type_counts.index, type_counts.values,
                   color=[NETFLIX_RED, ACCENT_TEAL], width=0.5, edgecolor='none')
axes[1].set_title('Count by Type', color='white')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Number of Titles')
axes[1].yaxis.grid(True)
for bar in bars:
    axes[1].text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height() + 30,
                 f'{bar.get_height():,}', ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=12, color='white')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(type_counts.to_string())

type
Movie      6131
TV Show    2676

3.2 Genre Popularity¶

In [22]:
# Explode genres so each title can appear in multiple genre counts
genre_series = df['genres_list'].explode()
top_genres   = genre_series.value_counts().head(15)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

colors = [NETFLIX_RED if i == 0 else '#444' for i in range(len(top_genres))]
bars   = ax.barh(top_genres.index[::-1], top_genres.values[::-1], color=colors[::-1], edgecolor='none')

ax.set_title('Top 15 Most Popular Genres on Netflix', fontsize=15)
ax.set_xlabel('Number of Titles')
ax.xaxis.grid(True)

for bar, val in zip(bars, top_genres.values[::-1]):
    ax.text(val + 10, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
            f'{val:,}', va='center', fontsize=9, color='#bbb')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

3.3 Ratings Distribution¶

In [23]:
rating_order = df['rating'].value_counts().index.tolist()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
fig.suptitle('Content Ratings Distribution', fontsize=15, fontweight='bold', color='white')

# Overall ratings
rating_counts = df['rating'].value_counts()
cmap_colors   = plt.cm.get_cmap('RdYlGn', len(rating_counts))
bar_colors    = [cmap_colors(i) for i in range(len(rating_counts))]

axes[0].bar(rating_counts.index, rating_counts.values, color=bar_colors, edgecolor='none')
axes[0].set_title('All Titles')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Rating')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Count')
axes[0].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
axes[0].yaxis.grid(True)

# Ratings split by type
rating_type = df.groupby(['rating', 'type']).size().unstack(fill_value=0)
rating_type.plot(kind='bar', ax=axes[1], color=[NETFLIX_RED, ACCENT_TEAL],
                 width=0.7, edgecolor='none')
axes[1].set_title('Movies vs TV Shows')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Rating')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Count')
axes[1].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
axes[1].legend(title='Type', facecolor='#222', edgecolor='#444', labelcolor='white')
axes[1].yaxis.grid(True)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

  cmap_colors   = plt.cm.get_cmap('RdYlGn', len(rating_counts))

3.4 Duration Analysis¶

In [28]:
movies  = df[df['type'] == 'Movie'].dropna(subset=['duration_int'])
tvshows = df[df['type'] == 'TV Show'].dropna(subset=['duration_int'])

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
fig.suptitle('Duration Analysis', fontsize=15, fontweight='bold', color='white')

# Movie duration histogram
axes[0].hist(movies['duration_int'], bins=40, color=NETFLIX_RED, edgecolor='#0f0f0f', alpha=0.9)
axes[0].axvline(movies['duration_int'].mean(), color=ACCENT_GOLD, linestyle='--', linewidth=2,
                label=f"Mean: {movies['duration_int'].mean():.0f} min")
axes[0].axvline(movies['duration_int'].median(), color=ACCENT_TEAL, linestyle='--', linewidth=2,
                label=f"Median: {movies['duration_int'].median():.0f} min")
axes[0].set_title('Movie Duration (minutes)')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Duration (min)')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Count')
axes[0].legend(facecolor='#222', edgecolor='#444', labelcolor='white')
axes[0].yaxis.grid(True)

# TV Show seasons bar chart
season_counts = tvshows['duration_int'].value_counts().sort_index().head(12)
axes[1].bar(season_counts.index.astype(int), season_counts.values,
            color=ACCENT_TEAL, edgecolor='#0f0f0f')
axes[1].set_title('TV Show – Number of Seasons')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Seasons')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Number of Shows')
axes[1].xaxis.set_major_locator(mticker.MultipleLocator(1))
axes[1].yaxis.grid(True)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"Movie Duration Stats:\n{movies['duration_int'].describe().round(1)}\n")
print(f"TV Show Season Stats:\n{tvshows['duration_int'].describe().round(1)}")

4. Trend Analysis ¶

4.1 Content Added Per Year¶

In [29]:
yearly = df.dropna(subset=['year_added'])
yearly = yearly[yearly['year_added'] >= 2010]

yearly_type = yearly.groupby(['year_added', 'type']).size().unstack(fill_value=0)
yearly_total = yearly.groupby('year_added').size()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(12, 9), sharex=True)
fig.suptitle('Netflix Content Growth Over Time', fontsize=15, fontweight='bold', color='white')

# Stacked area
axes[0].stackplot(
    yearly_type.index,
    yearly_type.get('Movie', pd.Series(0, index=yearly_type.index)),
    yearly_type.get('TV Show', pd.Series(0, index=yearly_type.index)),
    labels=['Movie', 'TV Show'],
    colors=[NETFLIX_RED, ACCENT_TEAL],
    alpha=0.85
)
axes[0].set_ylabel('Titles Added')
axes[0].set_title('Stacked: Movies vs TV Shows')
axes[0].legend(facecolor='#222', edgecolor='#444', labelcolor='white')
axes[0].yaxis.grid(True)

# YoY growth rate
growth = yearly_total.pct_change() * 100
bar_colors = [NETFLIX_RED if v < 0 else ACCENT_GOLD for v in growth.values]
axes[1].bar(growth.index, growth.values, color=bar_colors, edgecolor='none')
axes[1].axhline(0, color='white', linewidth=0.8)
axes[1].set_ylabel('YoY Growth (%)')
axes[1].set_title('Year-over-Year Growth Rate')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Year')
axes[1].yaxis.grid(True)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

4.2 Genre Growth Over Time¶

In [30]:
# Explode genres and merge year_added
genre_year = df.dropna(subset=['year_added']).copy()
genre_year = genre_year[genre_year['year_added'] >= 2013]
genre_year = genre_year.explode('genres_list')

# Focus on top 6 genres
top6 = genre_series.value_counts().head(6).index.tolist()
genre_year_filtered = genre_year[genre_year['genres_list'].isin(top6)]

pivot = genre_year_filtered.groupby(['year_added', 'genres_list']).size().unstack(fill_value=0)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 6))
for i, genre in enumerate(top6):
    if genre in pivot.columns:
        ax.plot(pivot.index, pivot[genre], marker='o', linewidth=2.5,
                label=genre, color=PALETTE[i], markersize=5)

ax.set_title('Top Genre Growth Trends (2013–Present)', fontsize=14)
ax.set_xlabel('Year')
ax.set_ylabel('New Titles Added')
ax.legend(facecolor='#222', edgecolor='#444', labelcolor='white', loc='upper left')
ax.yaxis.grid(True)
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mticker.MultipleLocator(1))
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

4.3 Monthly Seasonality¶

In [31]:
month_names = ['Jan','Feb','Mar','Apr','May','Jun','Jul','Aug','Sep','Oct','Nov','Dec']

monthly = df.dropna(subset=['month_added'])
monthly_counts = monthly['month_added'].value_counts().sort_index()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5))
bars = ax.bar([month_names[int(m)-1] for m in monthly_counts.index],
              monthly_counts.values,
              color=[NETFLIX_RED if v == monthly_counts.max() else '#444'
                     for v in monthly_counts.values],
              edgecolor='none')

ax.set_title('Content Added by Month (Seasonality)', fontsize=14)
ax.set_xlabel('Month')
ax.set_ylabel('Titles Added')
ax.yaxis.grid(True)

peak_month = month_names[int(monthly_counts.idxmax()) - 1]
ax.annotate(f'Peak: {peak_month}',
            xy=(monthly_counts.idxmax() - 1, monthly_counts.max()),
            xytext=(monthly_counts.idxmax() + 0.5, monthly_counts.max() + 20),
            color=ACCENT_GOLD, fontsize=10, fontweight='bold')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

5. Advanced Visualizations ¶

5.1 Genre × Rating Heatmap¶

In [32]:
top10_genres  = genre_series.value_counts().head(10).index.tolist()
top8_ratings  = df['rating'].value_counts().head(8).index.tolist()

heat_df = df[df['primary_genre'].isin(top10_genres) & df['rating'].isin(top8_ratings)]
heat_pivot = heat_df.groupby(['primary_genre', 'rating']).size().unstack(fill_value=0)
heat_pivot = heat_pivot.reindex(columns=top8_ratings, fill_value=0)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 7))
sns.heatmap(
    heat_pivot,
    annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='YlOrRd',
    linewidths=0.5, linecolor='#1a1a1a',
    ax=ax, cbar_kws={'label': 'Number of Titles'}
)
ax.set_title('Genre × Rating Heatmap', fontsize=15)
ax.set_xlabel('Content Rating')
ax.set_ylabel('Primary Genre')
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=30)
ax.tick_params(axis='y', rotation=0)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

5.2 Top 10 Content-Producing Countries¶

In [33]:
country_series = df['country'].str.split(',').explode().str.strip()
country_series = country_series[country_series != 'Unknown']
top_countries  = country_series.value_counts().head(10)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5))
colors = [NETFLIX_RED] + ['#444'] * 9
bars = ax.bar(top_countries.index, top_countries.values, color=colors, edgecolor='none')
ax.set_title('Top 10 Content-Producing Countries', fontsize=14)
ax.set_xlabel('Country')
ax.set_ylabel('Number of Titles')
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=30)
ax.yaxis.grid(True)

for bar in bars:
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height() + 10,
            f'{bar.get_height():,}', ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=9, color='#bbb')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

5.3 Dashboard Summary¶

In [34]:
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(18, 12), facecolor='#0d0d0d')
fig.suptitle('🎬  Netflix Content Analytics Dashboard',
             fontsize=20, fontweight='bold', color='white', y=0.98)

gs = GridSpec(3, 3, figure=fig, hspace=0.45, wspace=0.35)

# Panel 1: Type donut
ax1 = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 0])
tc = df['type'].value_counts()
ax1.pie(tc, labels=tc.index, autopct='%1.0f%%',
        colors=[NETFLIX_RED, ACCENT_TEAL],
        wedgeprops=dict(width=0.55, edgecolor='#0d0d0d'),
        textprops={'color': 'white', 'fontsize': 10})
ax1.set_title('Content Split', color='white', fontsize=11)
ax1.set_facecolor('#1a1a1a')

# Panel 2: Top genres
ax2 = fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 1:3])
tg = genre_series.value_counts().head(8)
ax2.barh(tg.index[::-1], tg.values[::-1],
         color=[NETFLIX_RED if i == 7 else '#555' for i in range(8)], edgecolor='none')
ax2.set_title('Top Genres', color='white', fontsize=11)
ax2.set_facecolor('#1a1a1a')
ax2.tick_params(colors='#aaa')
ax2.xaxis.grid(True, color='#2a2a2a')

# Panel 3: Yearly trend
ax3 = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 0:2])
ax3.plot(yearly_total.index, yearly_total.values,
         color=NETFLIX_RED, linewidth=2.5, marker='o', markersize=5)
ax3.fill_between(yearly_total.index, yearly_total.values,
                 color=NETFLIX_RED, alpha=0.15)
ax3.set_title('Total Titles Added / Year', color='white', fontsize=11)
ax3.set_facecolor('#1a1a1a')
ax3.tick_params(colors='#aaa')
ax3.yaxis.grid(True, color='#2a2a2a')

# Panel 4: Ratings
ax4 = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 2])
rc = df['rating'].value_counts().head(7)
ax4.barh(rc.index[::-1], rc.values[::-1], color=ACCENT_TEAL, edgecolor='none')
ax4.set_title('Rating Distribution', color='white', fontsize=11)
ax4.set_facecolor('#1a1a1a')
ax4.tick_params(colors='#aaa')
ax4.xaxis.grid(True, color='#2a2a2a')

# Panel 5: Movie duration
ax5 = fig.add_subplot(gs[2, 0:2])
ax5.hist(movies['duration_int'], bins=35, color='#555', edgecolor='#0d0d0d', alpha=0.9)
ax5.axvline(movies['duration_int'].median(), color=ACCENT_GOLD, linewidth=2, linestyle='--',
            label=f"Median {movies['duration_int'].median():.0f} min")
ax5.set_title('Movie Duration Distribution', color='white', fontsize=11)
ax5.set_facecolor('#1a1a1a')
ax5.tick_params(colors='#aaa')
ax5.yaxis.grid(True, color='#2a2a2a')
ax5.legend(facecolor='#222', labelcolor='white', edgecolor='#333')

# Panel 6: Countries
ax6 = fig.add_subplot(gs[2, 2])
cc = country_series.value_counts().head(5)
ax6.bar(cc.index, cc.values, color=PALETTE[:5], edgecolor='none')
ax6.set_title('Top 5 Countries', color='white', fontsize=11)
ax6.set_facecolor('#1a1a1a')
ax6.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=20, colors='#aaa')
ax6.tick_params(axis='y', colors='#aaa')
ax6.yaxis.grid(True, color='#2a2a2a')

plt.savefig('netflix_dashboard.png', bbox_inches='tight', dpi=150, facecolor='#0d0d0d')
plt.show()
print("Dashboard saved as netflix_dashboard.png")

Dashboard saved as netflix_dashboard.png

6. Business Insights & Conclusions ¶

Based on our complete analysis, here are the key actionable insights:

In [35]:
print("""
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║              KEY BUSINESS INSIGHTS FROM NETFLIX DATA            ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                                  ║
║  1. CONTENT MIX                                                  ║
║     • ~70% Movies, ~30% TV Shows                                 ║
║     • TV Shows are growing faster — higher engagement/retention  ║
║                                                                  ║
║  2. GENRE DOMINANCE                                              ║
║     • International Movies & Dramas lead in volume              ║
║     • Documentaries are rapidly growing (niche → mainstream)     ║
║     • Stand-Up Comedy is a low-cost, high-engagement category    ║
║                                                                  ║
║  3. CONTENT RATINGS                                              ║
║     • TV-MA dominates — Netflix targets adult audiences          ║
║     • Expanding TV-Y7 / PG content → family market opportunity   ║
║                                                                  ║
║  4. PRODUCTION TRENDS                                            ║
║     • Massive content surge: 2016–2019 peak growth years         ║
║     • Post-2020 slight slowdown (pandemic + budget optimization) ║
║     • Clear seasonality: July & December are peak months         ║
║                                                                  ║
║  5. GEOGRAPHY                                                    ║
║     • USA is #1 producer by far                                  ║
║     • India, UK, Japan are rising international markets          ║
║     • Korean content (K-Drama) is a fast-growing subcategory     ║
║                                                                  ║
║  6. MOVIE DURATION                                               ║
║     • Median movie length ~100 minutes                           ║
║     • Optimal sweet spot: 85–115 min for completion rate         ║
║                                                                  ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
""")

7. Content-Based Recommendation System ¶

We build a TF-IDF + Cosine Similarity recommendation engine using the title's description, genre, and rating as features.

In [39]:
# ── Feature Construction ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────

rec_df = df[['show_id', 'title', 'type', 'description',
             'listed_in', 'rating', 'release_year', 'director', 'cast']].copy()

# ── Fill NaN values FIRST before any string operations ───────────────────────
rec_df['description']   = rec_df['description'].fillna('')
rec_df['listed_in']     = rec_df['listed_in'].fillna('')
rec_df['rating']        = rec_df['rating'].fillna('')
rec_df['director']      = rec_df['director'].fillna('')
rec_df['cast']          = rec_df['cast'].fillna('')

rec_df['cast_clean']     = rec_df['cast'].str.replace(',', ' ').str.replace(r'\s+', ' ', regex=True)
rec_df['director_clean'] = rec_df['director'].str.replace(',', ' ')
rec_df['genre_clean']    = rec_df['listed_in'].str.replace(',', ' ').str.replace('-', ' ')

# Weighted soup: description × 2, genre × 3
rec_df['soup'] = (
    rec_df['description'] + ' ' +
    rec_df['description'] + ' ' +
    rec_df['genre_clean'] + ' ' +
    rec_df['genre_clean'] + ' ' +
    rec_df['genre_clean'] + ' ' +
    rec_df['rating'] + ' ' +
    rec_df['director_clean'] + ' ' +
    rec_df['cast_clean']
)

rec_df['soup'] = rec_df['soup'].fillna('').str.lower().str.strip()
rec_df.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)

print(f"Recommendation corpus size: {len(rec_df):,} titles")
print(f"\nSample soup for: '{rec_df.iloc[0]['title']}'")
print(rec_df.iloc[0]['soup'][:300], "...")

Recommendation corpus size: 8,807 titles

Sample soup for: 'Dick Johnson Is Dead'
as her father nears the end of his life, filmmaker kirsten johnson stages his death in inventive and comical ways to help them both face the inevitable. as her father nears the end of his life, filmmaker kirsten johnson stages his death in inventive and comical ways to help them both face the inevit ...
In [40]:
# ── TF-IDF Vectorization ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────

tfidf = TfidfVectorizer(
    stop_words='english',
    max_features=15000,
    ngram_range=(1, 2),     # unigrams + bigrams
    min_df=2
)

tfidf_matrix = tfidf.fit_transform(rec_df['soup'])
print(f"TF-IDF matrix shape: {tfidf_matrix.shape}")
print(f"Vocabulary size: {len(tfidf.vocabulary_):,}")

TF-IDF matrix shape: (8807, 15000)
Vocabulary size: 15,000
In [41]:
# ── Cosine Similarity Matrix ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Using linear_kernel (equivalent to cosine similarity for L2-normalized TF-IDF)
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import linear_kernel

cosine_sim = linear_kernel(tfidf_matrix, tfidf_matrix)

# Build reverse title → index map (handles duplicates by keeping last)
indices = pd.Series(rec_df.index, index=rec_df['title'].str.lower()).drop_duplicates()

print(f"Similarity matrix shape: {cosine_sim.shape}")
print("✅ Recommendation engine ready.")

Similarity matrix shape: (8807, 8807)
✅ Recommendation engine ready.
In [42]:
# ── Recommendation Function ───────────────────────────────────────────────────

def recommend(title: str, n: int = 5, same_type: bool = False) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """
    Returns the top-N most similar Netflix titles.

    Parameters
    ----------
    title      : str   – Title of the movie or TV show (case-insensitive)
    n          : int   – Number of recommendations (default 5)
    same_type  : bool  – If True, only recommend from the same content type

    Returns
    -------
    pd.DataFrame with columns: title, type, listed_in, rating, similarity_score
    """
    title_lower = title.lower().strip()

    if title_lower not in indices:
        # Fuzzy fallback: find closest match
        matches = [t for t in indices.index if title_lower in t]
        if matches:
            title_lower = matches[0]
            print(f"  ℹ️  Title not found exactly. Using closest match: '{rec_df.loc[indices[title_lower], 'title']}'")
        else:
            print(f"  ❌  Title '{title}' not found in the dataset.")
            return pd.DataFrame()

    idx      = indices[title_lower]
    sim_scores = list(enumerate(cosine_sim[idx]))
    sim_scores = sorted(sim_scores, key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
    sim_scores = [s for s in sim_scores if s[0] != idx]   # exclude self

    # Optional: filter by same content type
    if same_type:
        content_type = rec_df.loc[idx, 'type']
        sim_scores = [s for s in sim_scores if rec_df.loc[s[0], 'type'] == content_type]

    top_idx = [s[0] for s in sim_scores[:n]]
    scores  = [round(s[1], 4) for s in sim_scores[:n]]

    result = rec_df.loc[top_idx, ['title', 'type', 'listed_in', 'rating', 'release_year']].copy()
    result['similarity_score'] = scores
    result.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)
    result.index += 1
    return result


print("✅ recommend() function defined.")

✅ recommend() function defined.
In [43]:
# ── Demo: Test the Recommendation System ─────────────────────────────────────

test_titles = ['Stranger Things', 'The Dark Knight', 'Breaking Bad', 'Bird Box']

for test in test_titles:
    print(f"\n{'='*62}")
    print(f"  🎬  If you liked: {test}")
    print(f"{'='*62}")
    recs = recommend(test, n=5)
    if not recs.empty:
        print(recs.to_string())
    print()

In [44]:
# ── Interactive Recommendation Explorer ──────────────────────────────────────
# Visualize recommendation similarity scores

def plot_recommendations(title: str, n: int = 8):
    recs = recommend(title, n=n)
    if recs.empty:
        return

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
    colors = [NETFLIX_RED if t == 'Movie' else ACCENT_TEAL for t in recs['type']]
    bars = ax.barh(range(len(recs)), recs['similarity_score'], color=colors, edgecolor='none')
    ax.set_yticks(range(len(recs)))
    ax.set_yticklabels(recs['title'], fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlabel('Cosine Similarity Score')
    ax.set_title(f'Recommendations for: "{title}"', fontsize=13)
    ax.xaxis.grid(True)

    # Legend
    from matplotlib.patches import Patch
    legend_elements = [Patch(facecolor=NETFLIX_RED, label='Movie'),
                       Patch(facecolor=ACCENT_TEAL, label='TV Show')]
    ax.legend(handles=legend_elements, facecolor='#222', edgecolor='#444', labelcolor='white')

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

# Test it
plot_recommendations('Stranger Things')

8. Future Improvements & Extensions ¶

Here are ideas to take this project further:

Area Enhancement
Recommendation Use BERT / sentence-transformers for semantic similarity
Recommendation Build a collaborative filtering model with user ratings
Data Enrich with IMDb ratings, box-office data via API
NLP Sentiment analysis on descriptions to classify tone
Forecasting Time-series forecast of content additions using ARIMA/Prophet
Geography Choropleth world map of content by country
Deployment Wrap recommend() into a Streamlit / Gradio web app
ML Classification model: predict content type from metadata
